Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyzing the DECLARE Model The Constraints in the Model 1 init R Every trace must start with R Receive Claim This is reasonable 2 existence C Every trace must contain C Close Claim This is reasonable 3 responded existence E A If E occurs then A must also occur but not necessarily before This means evaluation cannot happen without an assignment This is reasonable but weak it doesnt enforce ordering 4 precedence C R R must occur before C This is reasonable but very permissive it only requires that a claim was received before it was closed but allows skipping all intermediate steps 5 noncoexistence E and C E Evaluate and C Close cannot both occur in the same trace This is the PROBLEMATIC constraint

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Identifying the Anomalies Anomaly 1 Contradiction between existence C and noncoexistence E and C with the intended process flow The intended process requires both E Evaluate and C Close to happen in every claim But noncoexistence says E and C cannot both appear in the same trace This means If a claim is evaluated E it can never be closed C violating existence C If a claim is closed C it was never evaluated E violating the intended business logic that every claim must be evaluated before closing Anomaly 2 precedence C R is too weak The intended flow is R A E P N C But precedence only requires R before C This means a claim can go directly from R to C skipping A E P and N entirely Combined with the noncoexistence constraint this actually encourages skipping evaluation since including both E and C would violate noncoexistence Anomaly 3 responded existence E A is too weak responded existence means if E occurs A must also occur somewhere in the trace but doesnt require A to happen before E The intended process requires A before E Anomaly 4 Missing critical constraints No constraint enforces that P Approve must happen before N Notify or C Close No constraint enforces that A must happen before E only that they coexist No constraint enforces the full intended sequence R A E P N C Anomaly 5 The noncoexistence of E and C directly contradicts the intended process The intended process has both E and C as mandatory steps in every claim lifecycle The noncoexistence constraint makes it impossible to follow the intended process

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Hypotheses 1 Misinterpretation of business requirements The noncoexistence constraint might have been intended to mean evaluation and closing should not happen simultaneously ie not in the same timestamp or not as a combined step but was incorrectly modeled as cannot coexist in the same trace 2 Incremental policy changes The model may have been built in stages Perhaps an earlier version required only R and C and later the evaluation step was added The noncoexistence might be a relic from a previous policy that was not updated 3 Incomplete data technical issues If the event log used to mine the DECLARE model contained traces where claims were closed without evaluation eg auto closed or fast tracked claims the mining algorithm might have discovered noncoexistence as a frequent pattern and elevated it to a constraint 4 Pressure for quick processing Under pressure to close claims quickly adjusters might skip evaluation The noncoexistence constraint might reflect this operational reality rather than the intended process effectively legitimizing a shortcut 5 Conflation of evaluate with close Perhaps in some historical process evaluation and closing were merged into a single step leading to a constraint that treats them as mutually exclusive alternatives rather than sequential steps 6 The precedence constraint being too weak might reflect that the model was built from a log where many claims were fast tracked R directly to C and the model captured this permissive behavior

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: SQL Queries 1 Find claims that were closed without any evaluation 2 Find claims where both E and C exist violating noncoexistence 3 Find claims where evaluation happened without a prior assignment 4 Find claims that went directly from R to C skipping all intermediate steps 5 Find claims where the full intended sequence was followed 6 Check if the noncoexistence pattern actually holds in the data Let me write these out properly
